grading.extracted_answer: 1. Represent both the initial tree and the final tree using adjacency lists. 2. Perform a post-order traversal on the initial tree to ensure that all merges occurring in subtrees are processed before the merges at the current node. 3. For each node u in the initial tree, maintain a list of its current children. 4. Identify the set of target children for node u in the final tree. 5. For each target child v of u in the final tree, identify which child (or group of children) from the initial tree maps to it. 6. Any children of u in the initial tree that are not explicitly listed as direct children of u in the final tree must be merged into one of the target children. 7. Specifically, for a node u, iterate through its initial children. If a child is not a direct child in the final tree, it must be merged into the first child in the initial list that is a direct child in the final tree (or the first available target child). 8. Use a queue to record each merge operation as a triplet (u, child_to_merge, target_child) in the order they are identified during the post-order traversal. 9. Output the sequence of operations stored in the queue.

Question ID: coding-B2-usaco-2023-us-open-gold-tree-merging
Title: B2. USACO 2023 US Open Gold / Tree Merging

Problem:
Reconstruct a valid sequence of sibling-merge operations that transforms an initial rooted tree into a final rooted tree.

Grading target:
Binary hidden-test acceptance.

Submitted final result text:
FINAL_ANSWER: 1. Represent both the initial tree and the final tree using adjacency lists. 2. Perform a post-order traversal on the initial tree to ensure that all merges occurring in subtrees are processed before the merges at the current node. 3. For each node u in the initial tree, maintain a list of its current children. 4. Identify the set of target children for node u in the final tree. 5. For each target child v of u in the final tree, identify which child (or group of children) from the initial tree maps to it. 6. Any children of u in the initial tree that are not explicitly listed as direct children of u in the final tree must be merged into one of the target children. 7. Specifically, for a node u, iterate through its initial children. If a child is not a direct child in the final tree, it must be merged into the first child in the initial list that is a direct child in the final tree (or the first available target child). 8. Use a queue to record each merge operation as a triplet (u, child_to_merge, target_child) in the order they are identified during the post-order traversal. 9. Output the sequence of operations stored in the queue. JUSTIFICATION: A post-order traversal ensures that we resolve all structural changes within subtrees before addressing the parent nodes, which is necessary because a merge operation at a lower level changes the child structure that a higher-level node relies upon. By processing leaves-to-root, we maintain the integrity of the tree structure during the transformation sequence.

Judge whether the submitted answer should count as correct. Do not penalize harmless formatting or extra explanation if the actual answer is clearly right. If the answer is wrong or the benchmark is not gradeable from the reference above, say so.
